title: Sharma v. State of Maharashtra
facts: This is a sample educational case. The accused was charged with dishonestly inducing a private bank to disburse a loan by submitting fabricated property documents. The prosecution alleged that the accused presented false title deeds for a property he did not own and thereby obtained a substantial loan from the bank. The accused denied the allegations and claimed that he was unaware that the documents were forged.
issues:
1. Whether the accused had the requisite dishonest intention at the time of submitting the documents.
2. Whether knowledge of forgery is an essential ingredient of the offence in this context.
3. Whether the bank's independent verification duty affects the criminal liability of the accused.
judgment: The Court held that the accused was guilty. The evidence on record clearly established that the accused had prior knowledge of the forged nature of the documents and had acted with dishonest intention from the inception of the transaction. The bank's failure to independently verify the documents did not absolve the accused of criminal liability.
reasoning: Dishonest intention must exist at the time of making the representation. The subsequent conduct of the accused — including his disposal of the loan proceeds and his failure to repay — was consistent with a pre-formed dishonest intent. The Court applied the well-established principle that a person cannot rely on the negligence of another to escape criminal liability for his own fraudulent acts.
significance: This case reinforces the principle that dishonest intention at inception is essential for offences of this nature. It also clarifies that the victim's failure to exercise due diligence does not extinguish the criminal liability of the accused who has acted fraudulently.

title: Patel v. Union of India
facts: This is a sample educational case. A group of undertrial prisoners filed a writ petition under Article 32 of the Constitution challenging the conditions of their detention in a state prison. The petitioners alleged that they had been in custody for periods far exceeding the maximum sentence that could have been imposed on them, that the prison was severely overcrowded, and that basic sanitation and medical facilities were absent.
issues:
1. Whether prolonged detention of undertrial prisoners beyond the maximum term of the alleged offence violates Article 21.
2. Whether the State is obligated to provide minimum standards of sanitation and medical care in prisons.
3. What remedies are available to undertrials who have been detained for excessive periods.
judgment: The Supreme Court held that the rights of undertrial prisoners under Article 21 had been violated. The Court directed the immediate release of prisoners who had served more than half the maximum period of imprisonment for the offences alleged. The Court also directed the State to submit a plan for prison reform within four weeks.
reasoning: The right to life under Article 21 is not limited to mere physical existence. It encompasses the right to live with dignity. Detention in inhuman conditions violates the core of Article 21. The State's obligation to protect the fundamental rights of prisoners does not cease merely because a person is imprisoned. Undertrial prisoners are presumed innocent and must not be subjected to punishment before conviction.
significance: This case is a landmark in prison jurisprudence. It affirmed that undertrials retain their fundamental rights and that the State has a positive obligation to ensure minimum standards of dignity in detention. It has been widely cited in subsequent cases involving prison reform and rights of undertrials.

title: Kumar v. State of Tamil Nadu
facts: This is a sample educational case. The accused was convicted by the Sessions Court for causing grievous hurt using a firearm. The accused had fired at the complainant during a property dispute, causing him to lose sight in one eye. The High Court confirmed the conviction but reduced the sentence. The accused appealed to the Supreme Court on the ground that the injury did not constitute grievous hurt.
issues:
1. Whether the permanent loss of sight in one eye constitutes grievous hurt as defined under the criminal code.
2. Whether the reduction of sentence by the High Court was appropriate in the circumstances.
3. What factors are relevant to sentencing in cases of grievous hurt involving firearms.
judgment: The Supreme Court dismissed the appeal and confirmed both the conviction and the modified sentence. The Court held that the permanent loss of sight in one eye clearly falls within the definition of grievous hurt. The sentence imposed by the High Court was not disproportionate given the gravity of the injury and the use of a firearm.
reasoning: The definition of grievous hurt expressly includes permanent privation of the sight of an eye. The use of a firearm aggravates the culpability of the accused. Courts must impose sentences that are commensurate with the gravity of the offence and are sufficient to deter repetition of such conduct.
significance: This case provides a clear judicial interpretation of grievous hurt involving sensory loss. It is useful for understanding how courts assess the gravity of harm in criminal sentencing and the relevance of the means used to cause the injury.

title: Singh v. State of Punjab
facts: This is a sample educational case. Following a legislative assembly resolution, the Central Government introduced a Bill in Parliament to carve out a new State from the existing territory of a larger State. The affected State Legislature passed a resolution objecting to the proposed reorganisation. Certain members of the affected State's assembly challenged the Bill in the Supreme Court, arguing that Parliamentary action without the consent of the affected State violated the basic structure of the Constitution.
issues:
1. Whether Article 3 requires the consent of the affected State before Parliament reorganises its territory.
2. Whether a Bill for reorganisation of States is subject to any implied limitations arising from the federal structure of the Constitution.
3. Whether the expression of objection by the affected State legislature can prevent Parliament from proceeding with the Bill.
judgment: The Supreme Court upheld the parliamentary action and dismissed the petition. The Court held that Article 3 does not require the consent of the affected State — it only requires that the views of the affected State Legislature be obtained. Parliament is free to disregard those views and proceed with the reorganisation.
reasoning: The Constitution uses the word 'Union' rather than 'Federation' advisedly. The Union's power to reorganise States is plenary and is not subject to State consent. The framers of the Constitution specifically chose not to give States a veto over territorial reorganisation. The federal character of the Constitution does not form a barrier to the exercise of powers conferred by Article 3.
significance: This case authoritatively settles the constitutional status of State objections under Article 3. It is essential reading for constitutional law students studying centre-state relations and the nature of Indian federalism.

title: Reddy v. State of Andhra Pradesh
facts: This is a sample educational case. The accused, an Indian citizen, was alleged to have committed an offence against another Indian citizen while both were temporarily residing in a foreign country. On their return to India, the prosecution sought to try the accused under Indian criminal law. The accused raised a preliminary objection arguing that the Indian courts had no jurisdiction since the offence was committed outside Indian territory.
issues:
1. Whether an Indian citizen can be tried in India for an offence committed outside the territory of India.
2. Whether the territorial jurisdiction principle bars prosecution when both parties are Indian citizens.
3. What is the scope of the phrase 'within India' as used in the criminal code's jurisdiction provisions.
judgment: The Supreme Court held that the accused could not be tried in India for the offence committed outside India merely on the basis that both parties were Indian citizens. The Court distinguished between offences committed by Indian nationals on foreign ships and aircraft (for which jurisdiction is expressly provided) and ordinary offences committed on foreign soil, for which territorial jurisdiction is the primary basis.
reasoning: The principle of territoriality is the foundation of criminal jurisdiction. Indian criminal courts have jurisdiction primarily over acts committed within Indian territory. Nationality of the accused alone does not confer jurisdiction in the absence of a specific statutory provision. The court noted that the Indian Penal Code and its successor the BNS are primarily territorial statutes.
significance: This case provides important clarity on the territorial reach of Indian criminal law. It is directly relevant to students studying the preliminary provisions of the BNS, the Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, and the principles of criminal jurisdiction.
